title: Making a Western deckle and mould from timber and mesh
subtitle: How to build the basic two-frame tool for hand papermaking
excerpt: The mould and deckle are the foundational tools of hand papermaking. The mould is a rigid frame covered in fine mesh; the deckle is a matching frame with no mesh that sits on top and defines the sheet edge. Both are simple woodworking projects using softwood batten and window screen.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923): Hunter's detailed account of Western mould construction from primary sources and his own workshop experience. The 1923 text is in the public domain (Hunter died 1966; his later Papermaking 1943 may still be in copyright, but the 1923 text is safely PD). This tutorial follows Hunter's description of the softwood frame construction with mesh facing.

glossaryTerms.definition: The mesh-covered frame that holds the wet fibres as the sheet forms. The ribs of the mould impart the laid lines visible in 'laid' papers when held to the light.

The upper frame, matching the mould in outer dimensions, that sits on top of the mould during the pull to define the sheet edge. The irregular 'deckle edge' of handmade paper comes from fibres that creep under the deckle as the sheet forms.
A mould covered in a plain-weave mesh rather than the parallel wire construction of a laid mould. Produces a smooth sheet with no visible waterlines.

content.text: set is available from bookbinding and papermaking suppliers. A home-built version needs only timber batten, window screen mesh, and waterproof glue. The functional requirements are straightforward: the frame must be rigid, the mesh must be taut and fine enough to hold very short fibres, and the deckle must fit the mould snugly without gaps. This tutorial describes a

attrs.title: Papermaking blender or pulp preparation safety
attrs.body: Keep the blender unplugged when loading fibre or rinsing it out. The blade is sharp; do not reach into the blender jar while it is plugged in. Drain the vat before reaching in with both hands.

items.name: Softwood batten, 25 × 25 mm, 3-4 m length
items.notes: Pine or spruce. Enough for both frames. The wood must be straight and dry.

items.name: Window screen mesh (fibreglass or aluminium)
items.notes: The fibreglass type (sold for insect screens) is easier to handle than aluminium, which creases. Mesh count: 14-20 wires per 25 mm is the right range.

items.name: Waterproof wood glue
items.notes: For the frame joints. PVA is not waterproof enough, use an exterior-grade or boat-building adhesive.

items.name: Staple gun and staples
items.notes: For attaching mesh to the frame. 10-12 mm staples.

items.name: Waterproof tape
items.notes: To seal the frame edges over the mesh staples.

content.text: Cut and join the frame members

Cut four pieces of batten for the mould frame and four for the deckle frame. Each frame is a simple butt-jointed rectangle, glued and left overnight before attaching the mesh. For an A4 mould: inner dimensions 297 × 210 mm (the actual sheet size). Outer dimensions will be the inner size plus two frame widths on each side.

Cut the mesh 50 mm larger than the frame on all sides. Lay the mesh over the frame and staple one long side, pulling the mesh just taut (not drumhead tight, a little tension is sufficient). Staple the opposite long side, pulling the mesh evenly to remove wrinkles. Then staple the short sides. Trim the excess mesh flush with the frame edges. Seal all staples with waterproof tape applied in a single continuous strip around all four edges.

Place the deckle on top of the mould with the mesh face up. The inner edges of the deckle should align exactly with the inner edges of the mould frame. Any gap allows pulp to escape under the deckle edge, creating irregular sheet edges beyond the normal deckle-edge effect. Plane or sand the frame members if the fit is loose. A slightly loose deckle can be shimmed with strips of felt or foam weatherstrip glued to the underside.
